coredata.dc:description:
Background The discharge of large volumes of oily wastewater from industries has been a serious global concern. In recent years, membrane filtration has been considered an effective method for treating oily wastewater. However, membrane fouling is a major challenge inherent to this approach.
Methods This study integrated zwitterionic carbon quantum dots (ZQDs) into a tannic acid-(3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (TA-APTES) coating on cellulose acetate (CA) tubular membranes to explore the potential for improve the hydrophilicity and anti-fouling properties of the membrane. The CA tubular membrane was fabricated by dry/wet spinning method. A green and one-step synthesis of TA-APTES with ZQDs was utilized to modify the surface of the CA pristine membrane.
Significant findings Incorporating ZQDs enhanced the membrane's hydrophilicity. Results showed that the modified membrane with ZQDs (CA/T0.4A+ZQDs) displayed super hydrophilicity with a water contact angle of 12.53 ± 0.9°, pure water permeance of 987.13 ± 57.9 L m-2 h-1 bar-1, diesel-water emulsion permeance of 577.10 ± 64.7 L m-2 h-1 bar-1, and enhanced oleophobicity with oil rejection rate of > 99 %. Furthermore, the CA/T0.4A+ZQDs membrane showed the best anti-fouling property, with the highest flux recovery ratio (FRR), lowest irreversible fouling ratio (Rir,1) of 94.98 % and 5.05 %, respectively and mechanical stability after 5 cycles of oil-water separation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The discharge of large volumes of oily wastewater from industries has been a serious global concern. In recent years, membrane filtration has been considered an effective method for treating oily wastewater. However, membrane fouling is a major challenge inherent to this approach.

This study integrated zwitterionic carbon quantum dots (ZQDs) into a tannic acid-(3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (TA-APTES) coating on cellulose acetate (CA) tubular membranes to explore the potential for improve the hydrophilicity and anti-fouling properties of the membrane. The CA tubular membrane was fabricated by dry/wet spinning method. A green and one-step synthesis of TA-APTES with ZQDs was utilized to modify the surface of the CA pristine membrane.

Incorporating ZQDs enhanced the membrane's hydrophilicity. Results showed that the modified membrane with ZQDs (CA/T0.4A+ZQDs) displayed super hydrophilicity with a water contact angle of 12.53 ± 0.9°, pure water permeance of 987.13 ± 57.9 L m-2 h-1 bar-1, diesel-water emulsion permeance of 577.10 ± 64.7 L m-2 h-1 bar-1, and enhanced oleophobicity with oil rejection rate of > 99 %. Furthermore, the CA/T0.4A+ZQDs membrane showed the best anti-fouling property, with the highest flux recovery ratio (FRR), lowest irreversible fouling ratio (Rir,1) of 94.98 % and 5.05 %, respectively and mechanical stability after 5 cycles of oil-water separation.

ce:section.ce:para: One severe problem brought about by rapid industrialization and population growth is the shortage of clean water supply [1,2]. Oily wastewater, mainly discharged from industries such as petrochemicals, food, pharmaceuticals, oil and gas, etc., continuously contributes to the scarcity of water supply as it affects groundwater and other water sources. Moreover, residential and urban runoffs from the streets and canals carry oil from domestic sources [3,4]. Oily wastewater is also considered a primary pollutant in several aquatic ecosystems [5–7] and is regarded as one of the most potent and hazardous wastewater sources polluting the environment [8,9]. Hence, the treatment of oily wastewater is a significant global concern.

Conventional methods in treating oily wastewater include solvent extraction, floatation, centrifugation, pyrolysis, incineration, and solidification [3,10–12]. However, these techniques are inefficient due to operational difficulties, high operating costs, the release of secondary pollutants, and low treatment efficiency. Because of these limitations, the application of membrane filtration as a more suitable treatment method has been continuously explored [13,14]. Membrane filtration involves minimal chemical use, low energy requirements, and simple processes [13] making it more efficient than conventional techniques [14–16]. In this separation technology, the membrane acts as a semi-permeable layer between two phases and regulates transportation between each layer [17].

Cellulose acetate (CA) is a cellulose and polymeric membrane derivative widely used in membrane filtration for treating wastewater due to its cost-effectiveness, biodegradability, renewability, and suitability for large-scale processing [18–21]. CA also offers unique and excellent mechanical and thermal stability. It possesses outstanding membrane formation properties, such as hydrophilic and hydrophobic functional groups that can interact with hydrophilic and hydrophobic environments [19]. Considering its properties, CA is one of the most common materials used to form membranes for water applications [22]. Additionally, CA is constantly applied in the preparation of ultrafiltration (UF) and nanofiltration (NF) membranes [23,24].

Nevertheless, using CA as a membrane material also involves challenges due to nonreactive functional groups, inadequate thermal and chemical resistance, and low adsorption capacity that limits CA for adsorbing molecules [24,25]. Moreover, CA-based membranes exhibit poor flux caused by high flow resistance induced by the dense and thick layer and low membrane porosity [24,25]. Such limitations led to significant interest in modifying membranes, aiming to enhance separation efficiency by improving the membrane flux and selectivity [24].

The surface of the membrane is crucial to its performance since it is the first point of contact with the feed [26,27]. Such can be modified by surface coating to improve the membrane's hydrophilicity, roughness, and morphology[27,28]. One substance that attracts interest in membrane surface coating is tannic acid (TA) [29,30]. It is a plant polyphenol containing five ester groups linked covalently to the molecule's center glucose core [29,31,32]. TA is one of the most well-known polyphenols that gained widespread attention in membrane societies due to its favorable properties, like the adhering ability to different kinds of substrates—both organic and inorganic [29], hydrophilicity [33], and hydrophobicity [34,35] due to the presence of amphiphilic functional groups. With numerous hydroxyl groups, it can be directly utilized to improve membrane surface wettability and hydrophilicity [36]. TA is also eco-friendly, non-toxic [37,38], low-cost [39], and readily available [29,30,33,36].

Surface modification using TA can be done through a simple and fast process where TA is co-deposited with another component, such as (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES) on a membrane surface [29,40,41]. Researchers show great interest in TA–APTES coating owing to its compatibility with polymeric membranes, uncomplicated preparation conditions, and other outstanding advantages in membrane surface modification [26,42,43]. APTES also provides cross-linking units that aid in the mechanical stability and strength of the resulting membrane [44]. TA-APTES coating was found compatible with hydrophobic membrane materials such as polypropylene (PP), nylon, polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE), and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) [26,43]. Additionally, TA-APTES coating can be easily formed under mild conditions. With its unique structural properties, this coating can be vital in designing superhydrophobic, catalytic, and adsorbent functional materials [45,46]. Furthermore, TA and APTES are cost-effective and readily available [26,43].

Another critical challenge in using CA membranes is their susceptibility to fouling. Fouling causes the membrane's poor rejection rates and flux by accumulating the materials rejected on the surface or membrane pores [47,48]. In treating oily wastewater, membrane fouling can be attributed to the adhesion of oily impurities on the membrane surface, which is caused by the interaction between the oil droplets and the membrane [14]. An emerging effective technique to control fouling is modifying the membrane surface using the zwitterionic carbon quantum dots (ZQDs) [48–50]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are carbon skeleton structures nanomaterials with size of less than 10 nm [51–53]. Since its discovery in 2004 [51,54], the potential of CQDs for numerous applications has been widely explored owing to the abundance of their hydrophilic surface functional groups and nanometer size [51]. Zwitterionic modification of quantum dots is a technique employed in membrane separation technology to improve membrane hydrophilicity and antifouling properties [55,56]. Because of their high affinity for water molecules, zwitterions can enhance the hydrophilicity of membranes and improve their antifouling characteristics by engaging in electrostatic interactions. Moreover, techniques like surface coating, surface grafting, and biomimetic adhesion are used to introduce zwitterions into membranes to achieve zwitterionic modification [56].

This study aims to enhance the separation performance and to improve the antifouling properties of the pristine CA tubular membrane through surface modification via co-deposition of TA and APTES with the incorporation of ZQDS on the membrane surface to create super hydrophilic and oleophobic layers. The surface modification involved synthesizing TA-APTES coating formed through Michael addition and Schiff base reactions using an alkaline tris-HCl buffer solution. The modified membrane, with the highest hydrophilicity, permeance, and separation efficiency, was further modified by introducing ZQDs to the surface coating system. To produce ZQDS, CQDs were initially synthesized and then reacted with N, N′-diisopropyl carbodiimide (DIC), and N‑hydroxy succinimide (NHS). To dominate the transition of the CQDs, N, N’-dimethylethylenediamine (DMEDA) was introduced, and finally, to form the ZQDs, a ring-opening reaction was facilitated by adding 3,2-dioxathiolane 2,2-dioxide (DTD). The study was initially conducted by fabricating a CA pristine tubular membrane utilizing the dry-wet spinning technique. Then, the surface was modified using the modification system with ZQDs. Afterward, separation performance and anti-fouling properties and membrane stability were examined. Modifying the pristine membrane through surface modification using TA-APTES with ZQDs led to significant improvements in hydrophilicity, oleophobicity, separation efficiency, and antifouling properties.

A polyester (PET) tubular substrate with 1.8 mm thickness was acquired from King Membrane Energy Technology Inc. (Tainan, Taiwan). Cellulose acetate powder (CA-394–60S) was purchased from Eastman Chemical Company (Palo Alto, CA, USA). N-methyl-2-pyrolidone (NMP, 99 %) and dodecane (95 %) were procured from Tedia Company, Inc. (Fairfield, OH, USA). Citric acid (anhydrous, 99.5 %), sodium lauryl sulfate (SDS), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were acquired from Showa Chemical Co. Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). Hydrochloric acid (HCl, analytical grade) was obtained from Aencore Chemical Pty., Ltd. (Melbourne, Australia). N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), TA, and APTES (98 %) were bought from Alfa Aesar Co. (Heysham, LA3 2XY, England). N, N′-diisopropylcarbodiimide (DIC, 98 %) was purchased from Macklin Inc. (Shanghai, China). Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris, 99 %), 3,2-dioxathiolane 2,2-dioxide (DTD, 96 %), and N, N’-dimethylethylenediamine (DMEDA) were acquired from Thermo-Fischer Scientific Inc. Ethanol (95 %), and glycerin were procured from Echo Chemical Co. Ltd. (Tainan, Taiwan). Various oils for different oil-water emulsions, such as canola oil, engine oil, and diesel, were purchased from Weiyi Enterprises Company Ltd. (Kaohsiung, Taiwan), Exxon Mobil Corporation (Spring, TX, United States), and CPC Corporation (Kaohsiung, Taiwan), respectively.

CQDs were synthesized through direct pyrolysis of citric acid [56,57,58]. 2 g of anhydrous citric acid were placed in a Teflon-lined hydrothermal autoclave reactor. The autoclave reactor was placed inside a 210 °C preheated oven for 3 h for pyrolysis. The successful formation of CQDs was signified by the transformation of white anhydrous citric acid into paste-like orange liquid. The synthesized CQDs were mixed with deionized water and neutralized to pH=7 using 1 M NaOH. The volume of the CQDs solution was then adjusted to 100 ml using distilled water. Afterward, the CQDs solution was purified through a dialysis tube for 6 h with deionized water, which was changed every hour.

The zwitterionic carbon quantum dots (ZQDs) were synthesized from CQDs according to the method described in our previous study [56] as shown in Figure S1. The CQDs solution was first transformed into tertiary amine transitional carbon quantum dots (TQDs). A solution containing 0.8 wt% CQDs solution, 0.8 wt% DIC, and 0.64 wt% NHS was mixed in a container using a magnetic stirrer for 4 h at room temperature. After that, the unnecessary by-product was filtered and removed. DMEDA (1.6 wt%) was then added to the filtered solution and stirred for 45 min at room temperature to form the TQDs solution. Afterward, the solution was adjusted to pH = 7 using 0.1 M HCl. The neutralized TQDs solution was purified through 6 h dialysis, where the water was changed every hour. The dialysis of the TQDs solution was also done to stop the reaction of the added amine. The resulting TQDs solution was dried using a rotary evaporator and freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain the dried TQDs powder. The ZQDs solution was then prepared by mixing 0.5 g TQDs powder and 0.08 g sulfate DTD in a 50 mL ethanol/water solution with a 1:1 v/v% ratio for 11 h at room temperature (Figure S1). The ZQDs solution was purified through 30-minute dialysis, where water was changed every 10 min. To obtain ZQDs powder, the ZQDs solution was dried using a rotary evaporator and freeze-dried for 24 h

The dry-wet spinning method fabricated the pristine CA (15 wt%) membrane. A dope solution was prepared by mixing 37.5 g CA with 212.5 g NMP solution. After the complete dissolution of CA to the NMP solution, the CA/NMP mixture was degassed for at least 12 h. The CA tubular membrane was fabricated using the setup shown in Figure S2 at a 1.0 m/min speed to produce a combined membrane thickness (CA and PET substrate) of 225 μm. The fabricated tubular membrane was washed with water overnight to remove the excess NMP and stored in DI water before the characterization and performance test.

Several portions of 100 mL Tris–HCl (pH = 8.5) buffer were prepared and subsequently added with different amounts of TA and a fixed amount (1 mL) of APTES (Table 1 ). Pristine membranes were immersed separately into the prepared solutions for 12 h to form CA/TA-modified membranes. To prepare the modified tubular membrane with ZQDs (CA/TA+ZQDs), 1000 ppm ZQDs powder [49,56] (based on the CA concentration) was dispersed in a 100 mL buffer solution for 30 min using a sonicator. A fixed amount of TA (0.4 g) and APTES (1 mL) were sequentially added to the solution. Clean, pristine CA membranes were immersed in the coating solution with ZQDs (TA-APTES+ZQDs) at room temperature for a 12 h reaction. The prepared surface-modified membranes with ZQDs were washed with DI water multiple times to remove the excess TA-APTES. Finally, the modified tubular membranes were stored in DI water for characterization and performance tests. The compositions of the CA membrane and the coating solutions used for the modified membranes are listed in Table 1.

X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS, ThermoFisher Scientific Inc., United Kingdom) was used to analyze membranes' chemical and elemental compositions. Attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR, Perkin Elmer MA, USA) was used to analyze the chemical composition and functional groups of the quantum dots (QDs) and the membranes. The charges of the pristine and modified membranes were analyzed using a zeta potential analyzer (SurPASS 3, Anton Paar).

Field emission scanning electron microscopy with attached energy dispersive X-ray (FESEM-EDX Model S-4800, Hitachi Co., Tokyo, Japan) was used to analyze the membranes' surface and cross-sectional morphologies and elemental distribution. Before analysis, the membranes were freeze-dried and placed under SEM using carbon tape. In analyzing the cross-section of each membrane, samples were freeze-dried and fractured under liquid nitrogen.

The hydrophilicity, oleophobicity, and oil adhesion resistance of membranes were characterized and evaluated using a static water contact angle analyzer (PD-VP Type, FACE Automatic Interfacial Tensiometer, Kyowa Interface Science Co. Ltd., Saitama, Japan).

Four oil-water emulsions were prepared: diesel-, canola-, engine-, and dodecane oil-water emulsions. The emulsions were prepared by mixing a 1:99 weight ratio of oil and SDS to water and stirring at 1500 rpm for 8 h A reverse osmosis tube was attached to the tubular membrane to prepare the tubular membrane for the ultrafiltration performance test. The tubular membrane used for the ultrafiltration performance test has an effective length of 15 cm (Figure S3). The ultrafiltration experiment used a self-made laboratory crossflow setup (Figure S4). The pure water permeance and oil-water emulsion permeance, (J, L m–2 h–1 bar–1, LMH/bar) at 0.25 bar was calculated according to the equation,

where V (L) is the volume of the collected permeate, A (m 2) is the effective membrane area, t (h) is the operation time, and P (bar) is the filtration pressure. Additionally, to evaluate the separation performance of the membrane, a total organic carbon analyzer (Vario TOC Select, Elementar, Langenselbold, Germany) was utilized to measure the oil concentration of the feed and the permeate, in which the oil rejection rate (%R) was calculated as,

where Cf and Cp are the oil concentration in the feed and permeate, respectively.

A similar setup from the UF performance test was utilized to evaluate the anti-fouling properties of the membrane. The anti-fouling test cycle started with measuring the pure water permeance for 1 h with 10 min intervals. After pure water permeance, the feed was changed from DI water into oil-water emulsion, and the oil-water emulsion permeance was measured for 1 h at 10 min intervals. After completing the first cycle, the laboratory crossflow setup was cleaned by continuous washing for 10 min with 1000 ppm SDS in water, followed by another 10 min wash with DI water. The anti-fouling properties of the membrane were evaluated in terms of the flux recovery ratio (FRR, %), total fouling ratio (Rt ,%), reversible fouling ratio (Rr , %), and irreversible fouling ratio (Rir ,%) which were calculated according to the given following equations:

The chemical stability of the pristine CA membrane and modified (CA/T0.4A and CA/T0.4A+ZQDs) membranes was examined by immersing the membranes in DI water (pH = 7) for 7 days. The membranes were rinsed with deionized water and then freeze-dried before characterization. The chemical stability of the membranes was assessed by determining their water contact angle (WCA) and underwater oil contact angle (UWOCA), pure water permeance, oil-water emulsion permeance, and oil rejection rate after 7 days of immersion.
The mechanical stability of the modified (CA/T0.4A+ZQDs) membranes was investigated by subjecting them to a long-term oil-water separation test that measures the permeance every 1 h for each cycle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and chemical characterization of membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 1
a and b show the ATR-FTIR results for the pristine and modified membranes. As presented in Fig. 1 a for pristine CA membrane, a broad OH group was observed at 3450 cm−1. Characteristics peaks were also identified at 2935 cm−1, 1746 cm−1, and 1361 cm−1, corresponding to CH alkyl stretch, C=O carbonyl stretching vibrations, and CO-C ether bending vibrations, respectively [59,60]. Upon coating with (TA-APTES), a significant peak was observed in all modified membranes at 3178 cm−1, corresponding to the NH stretch attributed to the hydrolyzed product of APTES deposited on the surface of CA. It was also noticeable that the OH peak observed in the CA pristine membrane slightly broadened in the modified membranes, possibly due to the pyrogallol functional group of TA [61]. As shown in Fig. 1 b, new peaks at 1580 cm−1 and 1500 cm−1 pertaining to NH bending vibrations [61] and C=N bending vibrations, respectively, indicating the successful cross-linking of TA and APTES [42]. Furthermore, the peak observed at 1018 cm−1 can be ascribed to the Si-O-Si bending vibrations, which confirmed the reaction of TA and APTES through Michael addition and Schiff base reactions [42,62]. Additionally, the zeta potential (at pH = 7) of the pristine and modified membranes is presented in Fig. 1 c. The pristine CA membrane exhibited a negative charge of −29.88 ± 1.17 mV which can be attributed to the presence of numerous highly negative hydroxyl (-OH) groups. The modified membranes —CA/T0.2A, CA/T0.4A, CA/T0.6A, CA/T0.8A —exhibited a less negative zeta potential of −19.79 ± 0.85 mV, −19.17 ± 0.86 mV, −25.34 ± 1.54 mV, −28.07 ± 1.01 mV compared to that of the pristine CA membrane. The lesser negative zeta potential of the modified membranes can be attributed to the addition of positively charged APTES with protonated amine group
(
−
NH 3 +
)
. Results showed that the zeta potential of modified membranes with higher amount of TA (0.6g and 0.8g) were closer to the zeta potential of pristine CA membrane. The shift in zeta potential is brought by TA's numerous negatively charged -OH groups. Moreover, CA/T0.4A+ZQDs exhibited the highest negatively charged zeta potential of −33.66 ± 0.56 mV, which signifies the dominance of the numerous negatively charged carboxyl groups
(
−
COO
−
)
present in the carbon quantum dots. Fig. 1d presents the color changes of the membranes, from pristine CA membrane to modified ZQDs membrane. The images showed that higher TA concentration leads to a darker brown color of the modified membrane, signifying the co-deposition of TA and APTES.

Further analysis of the chemical compositions of the pristine and modified membrane was conducted using XPS (Fig. 2 a-d and Figure S5). In the wide scan spectrum of the CA pristine membrane (Fig. 2a), O1s and C1s peaks were visible and did not exhibit any detectable N and Si elements. Upon modification using TA-APTES, O1s, and C1s peaks were retained but were demonstrated in an altered C to O ratio. Additionally, the resulting CA/T0.4 exhibited additional N1s, Si1p elements. The N and Si elements were obtained from the hydrolyzed product of APTES. Upon incorporating ZQDs, the S2p peak became noticeable in the CA/T0.4A+ZQDs membrane can be attributed to the sulfate used in the synthesis of ZQDs. As presented in Fig. 2b, several peaks of C1s and O1s of the CA pristine membrane were observed which are split into three characteristic peaks. These characteristic peaks of CC (284.6 eV), OC=O (288.6 eV), and OC-O (288.6 eV) were clearly seen in the C1s spectrum.

On the other hand, for the modified membranes (Fig. 2c-d), the presence of C-Si, CN, and C=N functional groups was detected and was clearly seen at 284.5 eV, 286.1 eV, and 287.5 eV, respectively (Fig. 3 c-d). These peaks are to be attributed to the hydrolysis and oxidation of TA-APTES. Furthermore, the new characteristic peak of CN detected at 289.5 eV of the ZQDs-modified membrane indicated the successful incorporation of the zwitterionized quantum dots. These confirmed the successful deposition of TA and APTES via Michael addition and Schiff base reactions [42,63], as well as the covalent and physical bonding of the ZQDs to the modification system and on the membrane surface.

Elemental mappings on the surface and across the cross-section of the modified and pristine membranes are shown in Figure S6. There was a uniform distribution of C and O in the pristine membrane induced by the hydroxyl and carbonyl groups of the CA. The presence of N and Si elements was due to the surface coating of the membrane with TA-APTES. The modified membranes significantly intensified C and O because of TA's numerous hydroxyl and carbonyl groups. Upon incorporating the ZQDs, the uniform distribution of S element was detected, imparted by the sulfate group of the ZQDs powder.

Figs. 3 and 4 show the SEM images of the surface and cross-section of the pristine CA and modified membranes, respectively. Coating the pristine membrane with TA-APTES led to the appearance of various sizes of nanospheres on the surface of the membrane. As the amount of TA increased to 0.4 g (at a constant amount of APTES), the amount of nanospheres increased. It can be said that as TA self-oxidizes, it cross-links to the product of the hydrolysis and condensation of APTES under alkaline conditions (Tris-buffer solution) [64,65]. However, at 0.6 g TA (Fig. 3d), the nanospheres gradually decreased and almost disappeared at 0.8 g TA (Fig. 3 e). This can be attributed to the accumulation of the excess TA, forming a layer of TA nanoparticles on the top of the CA membrane surface.

To further investigate the disappearance of TA-APTES nanospheres at higher concentrations, the pristine CA membrane was modified using 0.7 g of TA. Representative SEM images of the modified membrane using 0.7 g TA are shown in Figure S7a and S7b. Compared to that of 0.6 g TA, the modification using 0.7 g TA shows deposition of fewer nanoparticles. Otherwise, CA/T0.7A exhibited more visible TA-APTES nanospheres than the CA/T0.8A modified membrane. This proves that at higher concentrations of TA, the TA-APTES nanospheres decrease leading to the formation of excess TA layer. The introduction of ZQDs led to more nanospheres (Fig. 3 f), suggesting the formation of more permeable membranes compared with ZQDs-free membranes.

The cross-section SEM images of the pristine and modified membranes are presented in Fig. 4. The results showed that the modified membranes exhibited more porous structures than the pristine CA membrane. The modified membranes (Fig. 4 b-f) that were modified using substances that contain more hydrophilic functional groups resulted in a more porous surface. While the modified membranes contained carboxylic, hydroxyl, amine groups, and anionic and cationic groups for ZQDs-modified membranes, the pristine CA membrane contained only carboxylic and hydroxyl groups. Additionally, the results obtained from the cross-sectional SEM analysis were consistent with those observed with the surface SEM images in Fig. 3. The porosity of the membrane coating gradually decreases upon incorporation of more than 0.4 g TA. This may be attributed to the quick formation of the TA nanoparticles layer instead of reacting to the constant amount of APTES.

The surface roughness of the pristine membrane and modified membranes were measured using AFM, and the results are presented in Fig. 5 (a-f) and Table 2 . The trend observed from the AFM images was consistent with that seen in the SEM images for all membranes. CA/T0.4A had the highest surface roughness of 106.33 ± 7.51 nm, while the ZQDs-incorporated sample (CA/T0.4A+ZQDs) retained the high surface roughness of 104.97 ± 4.41 nm. In comparison to the membrane with higher concentration of TA (CA/T0.6A and CA/T0.8A), CA/T0.4A had the higher surface roughness since excess TA layer was formed on the surface of (CA/T0.6A and CA/T0.8A), instead of the formation of numerous TA-APTES nanoparticles. This was supported by the surface roughness of CA/T0.7A that is shown in Figure S7c that is closer to the surface roughness of CA/T0.8A. Furthermore, the trend in surface roughness is reflected in Fig. 6
b and c. It can be deduced from the presented results that the low surface roughness of the CA pristine membrane (4.68 ± 0.11 nm) was significantly improved upon coating. It is to be noted that high surface roughness suggests excellent surface porosity that leads to membrane hydrophilicity and wettability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface properties and wettability of membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Contact angle measurements were performed to investigate the hydrophilicity and oleophobicity of the pristine and modified membranes. The results are presented in Fig. 6 a and b. The results show that the CA pristine membrane was hydrophilic, having a contact angle of 60.2 ± 2.5° The hydrophilicity of the unmodified membrane can be attributed to hydroxyl and carbonyl group compositions [18,66] of the CA polymer. Significant improvement in the membrane's water contact angle (WCA) was observed when the pristine membrane was coated with TA-APTES. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the modified membranes display a lower contact angle than the pristine CA membrane. The modified membranes without ZQDs—CA/T0.2A, CA/T0.4A, CA/T0.6A and CA/T0.8A—exhibited WCA of 32.7 ± 1.4°, 29.2 ± 2.5°, 22.7 ± 1.3°, and 18.1 ± 2.1°, respectively. This proves that the wettability and hydrophilicity of membranes improve as TA concentration increases. The hydrophilic groups in the coating, such as hydroxyl and amine groups, promoted membrane hydrophilicity.

Furthermore, TA-APTES coating on the surface acted as an excellent hydration layer [42]. The modified CA/T0.4A membrane (CA/T0.4A+ZQDs) became super hydrophilic with a WCA of 12.53 ± 0.9° when incorporated with ZQDs. Adding quaternary amine and sulfonate groups in the zwitterionic group of ZQDs promoted the super hydrophilicity of the modified membranes. Moreover, the surface roughness presented in Figs. 3–5 and Table 2 supported the results of the WCA measurements illustrated in Fig. 6 a.

Fig. 6 b shows the pristine CA and modified membranes' underwater oil contact angle (UWOCA). As seen in Fig. 6 b, the UWOCA of the CA pristine membrane was 125.8°, indicating the excellent oil adhesion resistance of the pristine membrane. The UWOCA of the modified membranes was consistent with the results of the surface roughness and SEM analyses. Upon modification, the UWOCA improved as the modified membranes resulted in a higher oil contact angle (CA/T0.2A: 142.9°, CA/T0.6A: 150.2°, and CA/T0.8A: 147.6°). These UWOCA signify that the oil adhered to surface of the modified membranes. The UWOCA results for the modified membranes (CA/T0.4A and CA/T0.4A+ZQDs) show that oil droplets cannot adhere on the surface of the membrane, suggesting an outstanding oil adhesion resistance property and the super oleophobicity of the membranes. The results shown in Fig. 6 b prove the effectivity of the surface modification when assessing the membrane's oleophobicity. The outstanding oleophobicity signifies that the hydration layer inhibits the adhesion of the oil droplets to the membrane surface [67].

Pure water permeance and diesel-water emulsion permeance were measured to assess the performance of the pristine and modified membranes using a self-made laboratory crossflow setup. As presented in Fig. 7
a, the pristine CA membrane exhibited a pure water permeance of 486.8 ± 35.8 L·m-2·h-1·bar-1. Modifying the surface of the pristine CA membrane with TA-APTES increases the pure water permeance of the pristine CA membrane. It can also be seen that CA/T0.2A, CA/T0.4A, and CA/T0.6A exhibit a pure water permeance of 572.8 ± 22.1 L·m-2·h-1·bar-1, 623.8 ± 22.4 L·m-2·h-1·bar-1 and 643.2 ± 13.6 L·m-2·h-1·bar-1 respectively. The modified membrane without ZQDs with the highest concentration of TA (CA/T0.8A) had the highest pure water permeance of 660.3 ± 29.1 L·m-2·h-1·bar-1. These results were consistent with the WCA of membranes (Fig. 6 a). The measured pure water permeance shows that the CA membrane improved hydrophilicity as TA-APTES coating introduced a hydrophilic functional group. As observed, increasing the concentration of TA led to increasing water permeance and membrane hydrophilicity. This is caused by the presence of the nanoparticles contributed by the coating, improving surface roughness.

As shown in Fig. 7 b, for the modified membranes without ZQDs, diesel-water emulsion permeance increases as the amount of TA increases up to 0.4 g, as observed with CA/T0.2A (350.7 ± 38.4 L·m-2·h-1·bar-1), and CA/T0.4A (387.9 ± 11.5 L·m-2·h-1·bar-1). Thereafter, a decrease in diesel-water emulsion permeance was observed when the TA concentration was further increased to 0.6 g, which is evident in the diesel-water emulsion permeance of CA/T0.6A (364.90 ± 39.2 L·m-2·h-1·bar1) and CA/T0.8A (352.0 ± 6.0 L·m-2·h-1·bar-1). CA/T0.4A exhibited the highest diesel-water emulsion permeance among the modified membranes without ZQDS. The diesel-water emulsion permeance of modified membranes was inconsistent with the trend observed in the pure water permeance, and water contact angle presented in Fig. 6 a. These results were consistent with the membranes' water contact angle (Fig. 6 a). This is because concentrations of TA greater than 0.4 g (CA/T0.4A) lead to the formation of a smaller number of TA-APTES nanoparticles, which lessens the attraction of water molecules to the membrane surface. Nonetheless, the oil rejection of the modified membranes was satisfactory (> 99 %) in comparison to the pristine CA membrane (97 %).

As shown in Fig. 7, CA/T0.4A had a higher pure water permeance, the highest diesel-water emulsion permeance, and excellent oil rejection than the CA pristine membrane. This sample was further modified by incorporating ZQDs into the surface coating of the membrane (CA/T0.4A+ZQDs). The introduction of ZQDs to the TA-APTES coating led to an excellent increase in pure water permeance (987.13 ± 57.9 L·m-2·h-1·bar-1), and diesel-water emulsion permeance (577.10 ± 64.7 L·m-2·h-1·bar-1), signifying an enhanced hydrophilicity and water affinity of the membrane's surface. The obtained oil rejection was > 99 %, indicating the efficiency of the oil-water emulsion separation. Adding the zwitterionic group containing quaternary amine and sulfonate to the hydrophilic TA-APTES coating enhanced the membrane's hydrophilicity and oleophobicity. This may be attributed to the bonding of water's positively charged hydrogen atom with the negatively charged quaternary amine of the ZQDs.

The anti-fouling property of pristine CA, CA/T0.4A, and CA/T0.4A+ZQDs membrane was evaluated through a 3.5-cycle diesel-water emulsion separation test. The results are presented in Fig. 8
a. After the 420-minutes separation test, the CA/T0.4A+ZQDs membrane was found to have the highest pure water permeance and diesel-water emulsion permeance compared with the pristine CA and CA/T0.4A. Generally, modifying the membrane leads to enhanced anti-fouling properties, which can be attributed to the hydration layer of the TA-APTES coating and the incorporation of ZQDs. The formed nanospheres on the membrane surface were composed of the TA-APTES nanospheres and ZQDs nanospheres. TA-APTES cross-linked with each other through a series of chemical reactions, such as Michael addition and Schiff's base reaction, leading to the formation of TA-APTES nanospheres on the membrane surface. The interaction of the ZQDs with the TA-APTES coating and on the membrane surface proceeded though covalent and hydrogen bonding causing them to embed on the membrane surface. The incorporation of ZQDs to the coating layer and on the surface also occurred through physical bonding, causing the ZQDs nanoparticles to be embedded on the membrane surface. The hydrogen bonding between the zwitterionic group and water further stabilizes the hydration layer formed by the ZQDs. Furthermore, the addition of ZQDs aids in preventing fouling due to its hydrophilicity, electrostatic repulsion, and the steric hindrances of the zwitterionic group that create a barrier and minimize interactions with the molecules present in the feed that contribute to fouling [68]. These are consistent with the findings presented in Fig. 8 b and Table 3 for the anti-fouling properties, such as flux recovery ratio (FRR), reversible fouling ratio (Rr,1), and irreversible fouling ratio (Rir,1) of the CA, CA/T0.4A, and CA/T0.4A+ZQDs membranes. However, it was also observed that the total fouling ratio of the modified membranes (CA/T0.4A: 42.02 % and CA/T0.4A+ZQDs:43.66 %) was higher than that of the pristine CA membrane (38.12 %). These findings can be correlated to the surface roughness of the membrane. Due to the significant increase in the surface roughness of the modified membranes as shown in Fig. 5 and Table 2, the surface created more sites for the foulants to adhere to the rougher surface as the oil-water separation progressed. Given the drastic surface roughness changes, the modified membranes were more prone to particle accumulation, possibly leading to higher fouling rates. The FRR improved as follows: pristine CA (79.29 %) < CA/T0.4A (91.17 %) < CA/T0.4A+ZQDs (94.98 %). The excellent FRR of the CA/T0.4A+ZQDs membrane is due to the enhanced hydrophilicity imparted by the ZQDs. Consistent with the UWOCA results in Fig. 6 b, CA/T0.4A+ZQDs had the lowest Rir,1 of 5.05 %. Moreover, the CA/T0.4A+ZQDs membrane exhibited enhanced anti-fouling properties, as indicated by the FRR after 3 cycles of separation tests (Fig. 8 c and Table 3). The FRR3 of CA/T0.4A+ZQDs (88.13 %) showed significant improvement from the pristine CA/T04A (85.36 %) and a dramatic improvement from the pristine CA membrane (61.11 %). This indicates that adding ZQDs in the TA-APTES coating on top of the membrane improves the membranes' hydrophilicity, oleophobicity, and anti-fouling properties.

The chemical stability of the pristine CA and the modified membranes (CA/T0.4A and CA/T0.4A+ZQDs) was evaluated by immersing the membranes in DI water (pH = 7) for 7 days. The membranes’ water contact angle and underwater oil contact angle after the immersion period were measured and the results are shown in Fig. 9
c. The pristine and modified membranes maintained their hydrophilicity throughout the 7-day immersion. The pristine CA membrane maintained its WCA of 59.0 ± 2.6°, while there is a slight increase in the WCA of the modified membranes (CA/T0.4A: 35.0 ± 1.7° and CA/T0.4A+ZQDs: 29.9 ± 2.7°). The oleophobicity of CA/T0.4A and CA/T0.4A+ZQDs membranes was preserved as the oil droplet did not adhere to the membrane surface after the immersion, as shown in Fig. 9 d and supplementary media. These results signify that the surface modification of the surface of the CA membrane can withstand a neutral (pH = 7) environment.

Furthermore, pure water permeance, emulsion permeance, and oil rejection were measured to assess the modified membranes' stability. Fig. 9 e and f present the pure water permeance and emulsion permeance of the membranes, respectively. As shown in Fig. 9 c, the pure water permeance of the membrane after immersion, shows little difference from the previous water permeance presented in Fig. 7 a. The pure water permeance of the immersed membranes were 482.95 ± 35.19 LMH/bar, 614.71 ± 70.71 LMH/bar, and 897.87 ± 11.58 LMH/bar for pristine CA, CA/T0.4A, and CA/T0.4A+ZQDs, respectively. Likewise, the emulsion permeance of the immersed membranes shows less to no deviation from the permeance of the original membranes presented in Fig. 7 b. The presented permanence in Fig. 9 c is still in the acceptable range as pristine CA exhibited 254.86 ± 9.5 LMH/bar, 333.86 ± 53.41 LMH/bar and 504.87 ± 8.21 LMH/bar for CA/T0.4A, and CA/T0.4A+ZQDs, respectively. Meanwhile, the oil rejection rate of the modified membranes after 7 days of immersion was retained at > 99 %. These findings prove that the modification system could withstand neutral condition through the co-deposition of TA-APTES and by incorporating ZQDs on the surface of the CA membrane.

The chemical stability test of the pristine CA and modified membranes was limited to only a specific condition (DI water, pH = 7) because CA membranes are stable at pH = 4 to 8 [69]. In strong bases, CA undergo rapid hydrolysis that breaks the ester bonds holding the acetate group. Similarly, in acidic conditions, CA undergoes a slower hydrolysis that leads to the degradation of the CA polymer [69–71].

Furthermore, the ZQDs-modified membrane (CA/T0.4A+ZQDs) was subjected to a long-term repeated cycle of oil-in-water emulsion separation, the result of which is presented in Fig. 10 . As shown in Fig. 10, CA/T0.4A+ZQDs was effective in prolonged oil-in-water emulsion separation. After the first cycle (60 min) of separation, the permeance of CA/T0.4A+ZQDs decreased from 100 % to 88.8 %. which increased again after washing (first cycle). After the second cycle of separation, permeance decreased to 84.55 %. As observed in Fig. 10, at the start of the fourth cycle to the fifth cycle, the initial permeance of each cycle became stable at 88 % to 89 %, and the permeance after every cycle for these two consecutive cycles finds were stable at 80 %. These finding suggests that the modified membrane could undergo repeated cleaning and rinsing without losing its super hydrophilicity and excellent anti-oil adhesion property.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation performance of different oil-water emulsion
ce:section.ce:para:
CA/T0.4A+ZQDs tubular membrane was subjected further to an oil-water emulsion separation test. Four oil (diesel, dodecane, canola oil, and engine oil)-water emulsions were used to test the performance of the CA/T0.4A+ZQDs tubular membrane for oil-water separation. The results are presented in Fig. 11
a. The permeance results show that the membrane had an efficient oil-water separation performance for each oil-water emulsion with a notable permeance. The highest permeance of 604.81 ± 36.9 L·m-2·h-1·bar-1 was observed with engine oil, followed by dodecane, diesel, and canola oil with 559.66 ± 53.2 L·m-2·h-1·bar-1, 577.1 ± 64.73 L·m-2·h-1·bar-1, and 555.34 ± 65.9 L·m-2·h-1·bar-1, respectively. Furthermore, the highest oil rejection of 99.8 ± 0.1 % was obtained with engine oil, followed by diesel (99.6 ± 0.3 %), canola oil (99.5 % 0.4 %), and dodecane (98.6 ± 0.5 %). It is also to be noted that CA/T0.4A+ZQDs showed an excellent separation performance, as oil rejection did not fall under 98 %. Moreover, the oil droplet size was measured, and the results are shown in Fig. 11 b and c. The results show that the permeate oil droplets decrease in size compared to the feed's. The permeate of all oil-water emulsions has oil droplet sizes ranging from 40 nm to 70 nm oil, signifying that an oil droplet in the range can still pass through the membrane [72].

Fig. 12 illustrates the difference between the feed (left) and the permeate (right) before and after the oil-water emulsion separation test. The feed was observed to have a milky white color, indicating the presence of emulsified oil in water, while the permeate was clear and transparent. The feed and the permeate sample images are further supported with the optical microscope (OP) images in Fig. 13 . The presence of oil droplets in the OP images of the feed indicated the presence of oil in the solution. In comparison to the OP images of the feed, the permeates show little to no oil droplets. The OP images of feed and permeate presented in this section revealed that a significant amount of oil from the feed was rejected after the oil-water separation test using the modified (CA/T0.4A+ZQDs) tubular membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative analysis of the existing membranes for oil-in-water separation
ce:section.ce:para: Table 4 presents a comparative analysis of the existing membranes employed for oil-water emulsion separation. CA/TA0.4+ZQDs exhibited high pure water permeance, emulsion permeance and oil rejection rate. It can be said that CA/TA0.4+ZQDs-modified tubular membrane could be applied for efficient oil-water emulsion separations.

The surface of the CA pristine membrane was modified using TA-APTES coating synthesized through a one-step and green codepostion approach in an alkaline condition using Tris–HCl buffer solution. ZQDs were further incorporated into the modified membrane, increasing the surface roughness and significantly enhancing wettability, hydrophilicity, oleophobicity, and membrane separation efficiency. The modified surface-coated membrane CA/T0.4A showed enhanced hydrophilicity and oleophobicity with a pure water permeance of 623.8 ± 26.4 L·m-2·h-1·bar-1 , oil emulsion permeance of 387.86 ± 11.5 L·m-2·h-1·bar-1 and an excellent oil rejection rate of > 99 %. The introduction of ZQDs to the TA-APTES coating led to the super hydrophilicity and oleophobicity of the membrane as CA/T0.4A+ZQDs exhibited excellent water contact angle (12.53 ± 0.9°), pure water permeance (987.13 ± 57.9 L·m-2·h-1·bar-1) and emulsion permeance (577.10 ± 64.7 L·m-2·h-1·bar-1). Furthermore, the CA/T0.4A+ZQDs membrane had an excellent antifouling property after 3.5 cycles of filtration test with flux recovery ratio of 94.98 %, reversible fouling ratio of 38.64 %, and irreversible fouling ratio of 5.05 %. Chemical stability test results prove that the water original pure water permeance, emulsion permeance and oil rejection of the membranes were retained even after 7 days immersion in DI water. Lastly, permeance recovery of CA/T0.4A+ZQDs was stable at > 80 % in the end of fourth and fifth cycles of separation. In conclusion, the surface modification using a hydrophilic coating of TA-APTES with the incorporation of ZQDs significantly enhanced the hydrophilicity and oleophobicity of the pristine CA membrane, and the modified membrane possessed excellent antifouling properties after being subjected to cycles of separation tests.
